perturbation.original_text: Samples agrees that, for a period of five (5) years (or such lesser period hereinafter provided for) following the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), he shall not, without the prior express written consent of the Acquiror: (i) except with respect to the Permitted Hotels (as defined below), call upon, solicit, divert, take away or attempt to call upon, solicit, divert or take away any existing or potential customers, suppliers or accounts of the Acquiror Companies, the -2- <PAGE> 3 Target Company or the Related Entities or their respective businesses in connection with any business substantially similar to the Business;
perturbation.changed_text: Samples agrees that, for a period of five (5) years (or such lesser period hereinafter provided for) following the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), he should endeavor not to, without something akin to the prior express written consent of the Acquiror: (i) except with respect to the Permitted Hotels (as defined below), call upon, solicit, divert, take away or attempt to call upon, solicit, divert or take away any existing or potential customers, suppliers or accounts of the Acquiror Companies, the -2- <PAGE> 3 Target Company or the Related Entities or their respective businesses in connection with any business substantially similar to the Business;
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'should endeavor not to' weakens the obligation. Adding 'something akin to' makes the requirement for prior written consent ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires contracts to be clear and definite. The terms 'should endeavor not to' and 'something akin to' are too vague to create an enforceable obligation, making the clause difficult to interpret and enforce under Delaware contract law.

Contracts Subchapter I. General Provisions Â§ 2701. Joint and several contracts. An obligation or written contract of several persons shall be joint and several, unless otherwise expressed. Code 1852, Â§ â1170;Â Code 1915, Â§ â2628;Â Code 1935, Â§ â3108;Â 6 Del. C. 1953, Â§ â2701;Â Â§ 2702. Assignment of bonds, specialties and notes. (a) All bonds, specialties, and notes in writing, payable to any person, or order, or assigns, may be assigned, or indorsed, and the assignee, or indorsee, his or her executors, administrators, or assigns, may again assign, or indorse the same, as often as desired. The assignees, or indorsees, or their executors, or administrators, may, in their own name, sue for and recover the money due on the bonds, specialties, or notes. All assignments of bonds or specialties shall be under hand and seal, and executed before at least one credible witness. (b) Assignors, or indorsers, or their executors, or administrators may not release or discharge any sum due by the bonds, specialties, or notes, after the date of the assignment, and no release, receipt, or discharge from him, her or them made after the date of the assignment shall be good or available. perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not' to 'should endeavor not to' significantly weakens the obligation, and 'something akin to' introduces ambiguity to the requirement for written consent. The Law Explanation states Delaware law requires contracts to be clear and definite, and these changes create vagueness, making the clause difficult to enforce. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on assignment of bonds and joint contracts, which is not directly relevant, the Law Explanation's statement about the need for clarity and definiteness in contracts under Delaware law, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, suggests that the modified text creates an ambiguous legal obligation as defined in the perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: (ii) hire or attempt to hire, for himself or on behalf of any other person, any present or future employee of the Acquiror Companies, the Target Company or any Related Entity; or (iii) own, lease, maintain, operate, franchise, license, manage, invest in or provide financing for, or give any advice to any person, firm, partnership, association, venture, corporation or other entity owning an interest in or engaging, directly or indirectly, in the management or operation of, (A) any Hilton Garden, Courtyard by Marriott or Doubletree Club hotel wherever located or (B) any other Upscale Hotel (as defined below) or Extended Stay Hotel (as defined below) located in the United States, Canada, Mexico or the Caribbean within a ten (10)-mile radius of any hotel now or hereafter operated under the "Wyndham," "ClubHouse," or "Homegate" brand, except for any such activity conducted for or on behalf of, or in conjunction with, any of the Acquiror Companies or, in the case of clause (B), Permitted Hotels (as defined below);
perturbation.changed_text: (ii) perhaps consider not hiring or attempting to hire, for himself or on behalf of any other person, any present or future employee of the Acquiror Companies, the Target Company or any Related Entity; or (iii) endeavor not to own, lease, maintain, operate, franchise, license, manage, invest in or provide financing for, or give casual advice to any person, firm, partnership, association, venture, corporation or other entity owning an interest in or engaging, directly or indirectly, in the management or operation of, (A) any Hilton Garden, Courtyard by Marriott or Doubletree Club hotel wherever located or (B) any other Upscale Hotel (as defined below) or Extended Stay Hotel (as defined below) located in the United States, Canada, Mexico or the Caribbean within a ten (10)-mile radius of any hotel now or hereafter operated under the "Wyndham," "ClubHouse," or "Homegate" brand, except for any such activity conducted for or on behalf of, or in conjunction with, any of the Acquiror Companies or, in the case of clause (B), Permitted Hotels (as defined below);
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'hire or attempt to hire' to 'perhaps consider not hiring' significantly weakens the restriction. Replacing 'give any advice' with 'give casual advice' adds ambiguity regarding the type of advice restricted.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable and clearly defined. Phrases like 'perhaps consider not hiring' and 'casual advice' introduce ambiguity, making it difficult to determine the scope of the restriction and whether it is reasonable under Delaware law. Delaware Courts may deem a non-compete agreement unenforceable if it's vague.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by changing 'hire or attempt to hire' to 'perhaps consider not hiring' and 'give any advice' to 'give casual advice'. According to the Law Explanation, Delaware law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable and clearly defined, and Delaware Courts may deem a non-compete agreement unenforceable if it's vague. The introduction of ambiguity contradicts this requirement for clarity, thus creating a legal contradiction as per the 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Samples agrees that no bond or other security shall be required in connection with such injunction. Samples further agrees that the Restricted Period shall be tolled during any period of violation thereof by Samples.
perturbation.changed_text: Samples agrees that the need for a bond or other security in connection with such injunction might not always be required. Samples further agrees that the Restricted Period could potentially be tolled during what might seem like a period of violation thereof by Samples.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'might not always be' regarding the bond introduces uncertainty about the requirement. Using 'could potentially be tolled' and 'what might seem like' weakens the obligation to toll the restricted period, making it subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Delaware Court of Chancery Rules do allow for discretion regarding bonds in injunction cases, making the agreement state that a bond 'might not always be required' is a misrepresentation of the law as applied to the contract. The rules allow the court to decide. Further, the statement that 'the restricted period could potentially be tolled during what might seem like a period of violation' adds an uncertainty that could conflict with the intended enforcement of an injunction by making it more difficult to prove any violation of such injunction.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandated that 'no bond or other security shall be required,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating a bond 'might not always be required,' weakening the obligation and creating a contradiction. While the Delaware Court of Chancery Rules may allow discretion regarding bonds, the change introduces uncertainty that the original text clearly disallows. This uncertainty affects the enforceability of any injunction. The law snippet lists amendments to Family Court Rules of Civil Procedure, and while it doesn't directly address bond requirements, the 'Law Explanation' points out that the court retains the discretion. The altered contract language undermines the definitive nature of the original agreement, making it harder to enforce and creating ambiguity where none previously existed. This ambiguity makes it difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: The parties hereby expressly agree that the consideration given and received pursuant to this Agreement shall, for income tax purposes, be reported as amounts paid for a covenant not to compete. The parties acknowledge that Samples shall be solely responsible for the payment of any federal, state or local taxes arising from his receipt of the consideration under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties hereby express a desire that the consideration given and received pursuant to this Agreement might, for income tax purposes, be reported as amounts paid for a covenant not to compete. The parties acknowledge that Samples will likely be responsible for the payment of any federal, state or local taxes arising from his receipt of the consideration under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'expressly agree' to 'express a desire' and 'shall' to 'might' makes the tax reporting agreement non-binding. Using 'will likely be responsible' makes Samples' tax responsibility uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal tax law regarding the characterization of payments in agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S. Code § 162(k)(1) addresses the amortization of goodwill and certain other intangibles in business acquisitions. Characterizing a payment as being for a covenant not to compete has specific tax consequences. By changing the language to reflect a mere 'desire' that the payments 'might' be characterized this way, the contract creates ambiguity as to the true intention of the parties, which could result in the IRS recharacterizing the payments. The statement 'will likely be responsible' could make it unclear as to who is actually responsible for the tax burden, especially if the IRS disagrees with the characterization of the payments.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 162 - Trade or business expenses | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 162 - Trade or business expenses U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) In general There shall be allowed as a deduction all the ordinary and necessary expenses paid or incurred during the taxable year in carrying on any trade or business, including— (1) a reasonable allowance for salaries or other compensation for personal services actually rendered; (2) traveling expenses (including amounts expended for meals and lodging other than amounts which are lavish or extravagant under the circumstances) while away from home in the pursuit of a trade or business; and (3) rentals or other payments required to be made as a condition to the continued use or possession, for purposes of the trade or business, of property to which the taxpayer has not taken or is not taking title or in which he has no equity. For purposes of the preceding sentence, the place of residence of a Member of Congress (including any Delegate and Resident Commissioner) within the State, congressional district, or possession which he represents in Congress shall be considered his home, but amounts expended by such Members within each taxable year for living expenses shall not be deductible for income tax purposes. For purposes of paragraph (2), the taxpayer shall not be treated as being temporarily away from home during any period of employment if such period exceeds 1 year. The preceding sentence shall not apply to any Federal employee during any period for which such employee is certified by the Attorney General (or the designee thereof) as traveling on behalf of the United States in temporary duty status to investigate or prosecute, or provide support services for the investigation or prosecution of, a Federal crime. (b) Charitable contributions and gifts excepted No deduction shall be allowed under
perturbation.justification: The original contract states the parties 'expressly agree' on tax reporting, creating a clear obligation. The changed text uses 'express a desire' and 'might', weakening this agreement to a mere suggestion. This introduces ambiguity regarding the intent of the parties regarding tax reporting. The phrase 'will likely be responsible' further weakens the obligation concerning tax responsibility. The law explanation indicates that this ambiguity could lead the IRS to recharacterize payments and creates uncertainty about tax liability, directly contradicting the original clear agreement. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly define these terms, the LLM explanation points to potential recharacterization by the IRS due to the ambiguity, implying a risk of non-compliance.
